sentences.text: All right.
I just-- very, very briefly.
This woman created an oasis for us in the eye of the storm.
There is a book that was published called which was before I made contact with the school.
And I became Known Survivor Number 172 when, by a fluke, I made contact with the school.
And it has become a very integral, important part of my life.
I'm back in touch with a lot of my friends.
The school was totally extraordinary.

As a matter of fact, the strange thing is that, in 1937, an American photographer went over, and when I went to the Holocaust Museum, I saw myself at the age of 15, 14, at-- in the Holocaust Museum on a small piece of tape that was made at the school.
It was-- we went to that school and we were among peers and we could talk freely and we had a great life.
We didn't want to go home.
We didn't want to go on vacations.
The school-- of course, it also helped that it was co-ed and for the first time I got to know boys.
There had never been any boys in my life.
But in general, we-- it was an extraordinary experience.
And as I say, when I first came to America, I did not want to talk German.
Refused to talk German as soon as I knew a little bit of English.
I learned English inside of two months.
And as soon as I could speak English, I refused to speak German.
At home, on the street, never.
I would never speak a word of German.
I did not want to have any contact with anyone from Berlin.
I walked past people in New York.
When I got old, I suddenly saw that there was a terrible void in my life, and I put ads in the the the the and of course, never got a reply.
The ads weren't big enough and they were lost.
And as I say, through a fluke, I made contact.
And I'm now in touch with former classmates, some of my best friends.
Some of them are dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Most of them got out.
There are now 187 people on that list, even though a lot of them have died.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Yes.
I loved school.
I loved learning.
I thirsted for knowledge.
And when my parents first told me that I was going to go to a private Jewish school, I said, no.
I would rather sit in the last row and have no contact than not go to school.
I know that sounds very, very impossible and hard for an American to understand, but you must understand that the Germans are very disciplined, very regimented.
We were brought up that way.
And beyond that, it was-- sure, we were-- it was a daily ache, hurt.
It wasn't easy.
But there were so many things that were so much worse that just going to school and not being able to talk to the-- to our former friends was nothing.
I mean, we saw people beaten to death on the street.
You know, I mean, Terrible things were happening.
They were making soap out of Jews, even then.
So all the people who say that-- that nothing happened before 1939, it's nonsense.
Utter, total nonsense.
We know about Ilse Koch, who-- who made lampshades out of Jews in concentration camps, and that's long before the so-called final solution and the gas ovens.
I mean, the concentration camps were going full blast in '37 and '38, before I left.
We saw people-- we knew of people who were arrested and who were beaten to death.
I-- I didn't witness-- I-- I once saw a man beaten.
I-- I saw people taken away.
But, I mean, everything else-- My mother's gynecologist was-- was married to a-- to a pure Aryan woman, and he refused to-- to divorce her.
He loved her.
And before they could get out of Germany-- he was set to get out-- he was beaten in his bed in front of her.
And I overheard, when-- when she came crying and told my mother, that she never realized how much blood there was in the human being.
That there wasn't an-- an inch on the walls, and the ceiling, and floor that wasn't spattered with his blood.
But, you know-- so going to school and sitting there, not being able to talk?
My god, that was nothing.
Were you ever afraid for yourself or your family?
I was afraid for my family.
I was afraid in-- in-- in-- my God, in 1936, coming back from a trip to Italy-- 1935, I beg your pardon. '36, I would like to talk about that.
In 1935, we had been in Italy for the summer.
And we came back and-- and-- it's a long story, but they took us-- they took my father and mother.
It was a dumb thing that brought them in, but they took my-- my-- my-- mother and dad inside to be interrogated and left me alone in the car.
And then I heard loud voices, and my mother crying, and my father yelling, and I was sure that any minute I would hear two shots.
And somehow or other, my mother talked them out of arresting my dad, and-- and they came back out and we drove away.
Yeah, there were many times.
There was a night when my father was late, and my father-- you know, it got later and later and later and my mother stood-- and I stood on our balcony, quietly, not talking.
And-- and I was scared to death.
And I finally-- I apparently said to my mother-- I must have been 12, 13.
I finally said to my mother, I said, when they arrest a man, do they call you and tell you?
And that was all I had to say to my mother.
I mean, she-- you know-- became ballistic.
And half an hour later, my father came waltzing in.
I mean, we saw his car driving up and my mother pulled me away so that he wouldn't see that we'd been standing on the balcony like Madama Butterfly and Suzuki, waiting for him.
And he had gone to his stamp dealer who had found a very, very rare stamp and he forgot about the time, and-- and he was late.
Yeah, we were always worried.
If somebody was 10 minutes late, when there was a-- a knock on the door, when there was-- of course, we lived with Damocles's sword hanging over us.
We never knew what the next day would bring.
I-- I-- I started-- it-- it brought to mind something else and that was two other things.
In 1936, we went to Berlin and-- we went to London, to England for the summer, and we had a sewing room in-- in our apartment.
You didn't go to a store to buy clothes.
All my things were-- were-- we had a seamstress who came and made my clothes.
My mother went to a regular tailor for her clothes, but for my clothes, the seamstress came.
And one night, after-- the night before the seamstress came, after dinner, my mother said, please come into the sewing room with me.
You've grown again and I've got to measure how long she has to let out the seam on the skirt.
And we opened the door to the sewing room, and there was my father.
And he screamed at us, and he said, can't anyone ever have a moment's privacy in this goddamn house?
And it was so uncharacteristic that we quickly closed the door and we looked at each other and we didn't say anything.
They were very difficult times.
I mean, everyone was stressed.
And that summer, we went to England for the summer.

And we got off the ferry in Dover-- from Calais to Dover-- and as we stepped off the ferry, my father unbuckled his belt, and he pulled out the belt, and he held it up in front of our eyes, and he said, now, I can tell you.
I want to apologize for yelling at you that night in the sewing room.
But this entire belt is lined with money.
And my mother looked at him, and she-- she didn't-- he said, I didn't want to tell you because you would have been nervous and you would have given us away.
And my mother had only one question.
What do you mean it was lined with money?
Where did you have it done?
And he said, I did it myself in the sewing room.
And my mother said, where did you learn to sew?
He said, what's there to sewing?
I went out and I bought a leather needle, and I bought some twine, and I sewed it.
I opened it up and I sewed it.
My favorite story about money is the story of the bicycle.
This kid who discovered that the frame of the bicycle was the exact size of the German mark.
Somebody I knew.
And he took and he loaded up the frame of his bicycle with coins.
Very heavy.
And every Sunday he went over a different border.
And every Sunday, he left all the marks over a different border in the Netherlands.
And then, when he had enough money, he left himself.
I want to make an important point about the Germans.
And that is that most Americans still, to this day, think that the Germans were stupid, or complacent, or greedy, and that because they couldn't take their money out, they didn't leave.
Nothing could be further from the truth.
Every German I have ever come across-- every friend of my parents-- tried to get out from 1933 on.
It was not that you couldn't get out of Germany.
You could get out of Germany any time of the day or night.
The doors were wide open.
Hitler wanted us to leave.
We could get on a train, we could get in our own-- get into our own cars.
We could leave Germany.
We couldn't take anything with us.
That was not the point.
The point was that the doors were closed on the other side.
Very few people know about the Evian Conference of 1938, where 32 countries congregated to decide how many Jews each country could possibly take.
It was a summit meeting.
Today it would be a UN summit meeting.
At the Evian Conference, at the end, 32 countries decided not one single country could take one single Jew.
We weren't wanted anywhere in the world.
I mean, it would take me an hour of this tape-- another hour of the tape-- to go through what it took to get into what country.
The entire world was closed.
But since we're in America, I'd just like to mention that America took in 19,000 Jews a year.
Now, from 1933 to 1939, when the war started, and then the borders were closed, and then the Jews were taken into concentration camps wholesale, no longer one at a time.
In those six years, America took in six times 19,000-- 120,000 Jews came in.
There were maybe 1,000 that came in under special circumstances.
They took in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people who were famous.
Famous artists, famous politicians, famous scientists.
The rich and the-- the rich went to Switzerland.
The famous-- the famous people and the people who could really do some good for America.
Other than that, 19,000.
Shameless for America to have only taken in-- at the time, you had to have an affidavit, and I have our affidavit, which I'd like to bring into the record.
An affidavit had to be posted by a blood relative in America.
You had to have a rich uncle in America, which I was fortunate enough to have.
He had to post a bond of $5,000 which, in the depression in America was quite difficult.
You could post it-- you didn't have to post it in cash.
You could post it in war bonds, in-- in government bonds, you could post it in the form of a mortgage on your house, which is what most people did to get their relatives in.
Still and all, for a typical family of four people, it took $20,000, which was a fortune in the early '30s, during the Depression.
You had to post somehow or other.
And the $5,000 was very simply that it took five years to become an American citizen.
And in those days, they figured that it took approximately $1,000 a year to exist, so that the Jews that came in under this quota would never become a burden on the government.
That we would not ask for welfare, like people do today.
We-- we had a rich uncle who kept saying, month after month after month, I have great connections in Washington and I have been told that Hitler will not last more than another two months.
This went on for five years until my mother went to Czechoslovakia.
My mother had a connection in Czechoslovakia.
She supposedly had a gynecologist there that she went to see.
What she did was she paid this gentleman in Carlsbad, which is now Karlovy Vary in Czechoslovakia, to send letters to America, and receive letters.
And he would make a phone call every time my uncle would write.
And finally, he gave us my-- the affidavit is dated January, 1938.
My father, because he was such a big shot in World War I and, as I said, was protected until the day we left Germany, was taken ahead.
It-- in-- in January of 1938, it took at least two or three years to stand in line to get a quarter number.
My father got the quarter within 24 hours, got the number, and we made preparations.
We packed up and we left Berlin.
Actually, in March, we went to say goodbye to my grandparents in Munich, and we were on a boat in-- on April 6th [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to come to America in 1938.
My father was a very unusual man.
In 1933, when we went to Denmark-- which I mentioned earlier-- with my Tante Greta, the great opera singer, we came back through Hamburg on the way home.
And the Manhattan-- the SS Manhattan-- one of the two great ships of the United States steam line, was docked in dry dock in Hamburg.
And my father talked his way onto the boat, and he picked out the cabin which we would deign to come to America on, because he wasn't going to come on a German ship.

He was going to be on an American ship just as quick as he could, as soon as we got-- I mean, little did he think, in 1933, it would take my uncle in Cleveland to wait five whole years to send us that darn aff-- affidavit.
And he picked out cabin number B23 and, sure enough, five years later, we came to America in cabin number B23, which was pretty amazing.
We came-- we arrived in New York and my father, of course, being the Prussian officer, was the first in line at immigration.
And first, he pulled me up to show me the wonderful Statue of Liberty, and he was the first in line.
And there was the immigration officer.
And he looked at the papers and he said, Friedsam?
Are you related to Michael Friedsam?
And my father said, naturlich.
Of course I am.
And my father spoke English.
He had lived in London for five years before the war.
And both my parents spoke English.
My mother had lived in Cleveland for a year, instead of going to finishing school, and my father had lived in London for five years.
So they both spoke English.
And he said, of course I'm related to Michael Friedsam.
It's only-- the Friedsam family is only one single family all over the world.
And he said, well, in that case, I welcome you to your new home.
And he went bang, bang, bang, bang and we were the first people-- I mean, we weren't first class on the boat, but we were the first, literally, the first people off that boat.
And my father, who had thought about changing the name Friedsam and Americanizing the name, said, I'm not going to change my name if the name is that famous in New York.
This little man, the immigration officer, was the first and last person that we ever came across who knew the name Friedsam, except that Michael Friedsam has a big statue.
He was a major philanthropist in New York City.
He gave his art collection to the Metropolitan Museum of Art, and he was a very important person, but nobody has ever heard of him today.

This was around the turn of the century or shortly thereafter, and the name Friedsam doesn't mean-- Yes, there is a Friedsam Carousel in Central Park, but very few people-- most people just call it the Carousel in Central Park.
They don't call it the Friedsam Carousel.
There is a sign, however, and I do have a picture of it, but I don't have it here.
Well--
There are a couple of things.
One of them I started to mention, and that, again, my father-- you could not take money out of Germany.
My father's regimental commanders, several of whom were shot by Hitler before it was all over, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] made it possible for us to take some money out.
We got a wonderful exchange of, for half a million mark, we got $7,000.
But it was enough to give us a start even though, when we came to America, my father got a job for $25 a week.
He was then 55 years old, and he got a job for $25 a week carrying refrigerators and stoves up and down four flights of stairs, which was not easy.
The first year in America was tougher, believe it or not.
The first year in America was tougher than all the years in-- in Berlin.
My mother and I never knew whether my father would come home or whether he would have committed suicide.
It was very, very tough on this very proud man.
One of the things that-- that came to mind earlier was that my father-- you could not just take your money out of the bank.
I mean, if you took more than 100 mark out of the bank, you had to account for what you bought with it.
And my father slowly, slowly, slowly saved a mark here, 10 mark there, 50 mark there, and he bought gold coins on the black market.
And when he had enough gold, he took it on the black market to a basement somewhere, where a man had set up a smelt.
And he said, come back in a week.

And my father came back in a week and he got a very, very plain bracelet that-- and the man said to him, I coated it with junk on top, but it's solid gold-- heavy, heavy, heavy-- as dense as I could get this wonderful, pure, pure, 24 whatever karat gold that my father had collected.
And when we finally-- we left that-- my mother wore that bracelet.
He wore the belt.
She wore the bracelet.
It was a nothing looking bracelet on the outside.
It was very wide, but it was matte finish.
Didn't look like anything.
We left it in England, and when we finally got to America, our friends sent us that bracelet.
And when-- when my father took it to 47th Street to have it appraised, and the man that he gave it to looked at the bracelet and said, what do you want with this?
And my father said, it's 24 karat gold.
It's melted down, pure gold krugerrands, whatever you want.
And he said, no it isn't.
And he scratched it, and it was nothing but iron underneath.
That is how the Germans cheated us until the last minute.
Nevertheless, I mean, Germans came to America by hook or by crook, and they became maids, and butlers, and they sold the jewelry.
We were lucky.
Our furniture-- as you look around-- I live in a museum.
I have things that have been in my family for 300 years.
The boat that our furniture and everything came out on, literally, was the last boat.
After that, if you wanted to take-- this chair, for instance.

If you wanted to take a chair with you-- and this chair has been appraised for, I think $1,000-- and they would appraise it for $3,000-- you had to give the Germans $3,000 if you wanted to take this chair with you.
But you couldn't take money with you, so a lot of people, even after us, got to take their furniture.
We got to take our furniture for free.
We arrived on the 16th of April, 1938, in New York City.
All right.
My mother's name was Matilda.
She was never called Matilda unless she was a naughty girl.
Her name was Tilly.
Tilly [?
Behr-Friedsam. ?] And my father's name was Guido Andreas Friedsam.
My grandmother was a very unusual woman.
She was-- among many other things, she had open house for the intelligentsia of Europe.
She was known all over Europe.
She was a suffragette.
She was a master chess player.
People flocked to play chess with her.
She was also an amateur painter, and Guido Reni was her favorite painter.
My father-- had the-- I mean, in America, or in Italy, you would called him 'Gwee-dough.' In Germany, it was Guido Andreas Friedsam.
Landau, Pfalz.
My father's family from [?
Coplands ?] on the Rhine.
We came in April.
I started school.
We lived with a cousin of my dad's in Hastings on Hudson.
He owed my father a fortune, so he allowed us to stay there for a couple of months.
My father had made it possible for him to come to America.
He was married to an American woman, and therefore he got into America.
I think he had American citizenship.
He worked for Ford [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Germany.
My father had-- he had never had any money.
My father had given him a lot of money, which he never got back, and we stayed with him in this beautiful house on Hastings on Hudson.
I went to school.
The first two months, May and June of 1938, I went to school in Yonkers-- Yonkers High School.
And it was like being in a dream.
I didn't understand a word.

There was some-- the class valedictorian and the most popular girl of the school-- there were three girls, who will never see this unfortunately, who-- I haven't the vaguest idea what their names were-- who took me under their wing and were wonderful to me.
And took me around, and took me from class to class.
They were absolutely fantastic.
I took French, English, German, and I learned German in two months.
I mean--
I learned English in two months.
The German teacher was very difficult to understand.
His-- his German was terrible.
But I learned English that way, and I just concentrated on-- I took a couple of English classes.
And I took the German class and I took French, and I must have taken American history or something like that, just to get a feel.
We spent the summer in Manhattan.
My mother had a friend who had been born in America, who got out in '36.
She was an American citizen.
And she always went away for the summer.
She had a country house, and we lived in her apartment, right off Central Park, on 68th street.
And we spent the summer there, and then my mother went looking and she found an apartment for us in Jackson Heights, which was an inexpensive-- rather inexpensive-- neighborhood at that point.
Not a fancy apartment, but enough for our furniture, which had been in storage all that time.
And we got to unpack furniture.
I went to Newtown High School in Jackson Heights, which then was the second-largest high school in New York. 10,000 kids.
I was overwhelmed.
I came from schools in Berlin that had 300 kids and here I was in school.
